en.extract:
The New Zealand grebe (Poliocephalus rufopectus), also known as the New Zealand dabchick or weweia, is a member of the grebe family endemic to New Zealand.


Taxonomy
Poliocephalus rufopectus is an aquatic diving bird in the grebe order.
Poliocephalus rufopectus (G.R.Gray) was originally described by the English ornithologist George Robert Gray as Podiceps rufopectus G.R.Gray, 1843 (in subgenus Poliocephalus). The type specimen was collected by British botanist and scientific collector Andrew Sinclair in New Zealand. P. rufopectus is one of two species in the genus Poliocephalus, with the other being the hoary-headed grebe (Poliocephalus poliocephalus) which is native to Australia.


Description
The New Zealand grebe has dark brown plumage, a small black head with fine silver feathers, a black pointed bill and characteristic yellow eyes. It grows to about 29 cm (11 in), and weighs about 250 g (8.8 oz). Non-breeding individuals have a paler plumage and females tend to be a bit lighter, smaller and have a slightly shorter bill than males.


Distribution and habitat
This grebe species inhabits mainly shallow freshwater lakes, ponds and sheltered inlets.
Currently it is found mainly in the North Island, where it is well distributed on the coastal lakes of the West coast from North Cape to Pukekohe and from Taranaki to Paraparaumu, as well as on ponds of the Volcanic Plateau, Gisborne, Hawkes Bay and the Wairarapa. 
Formerly this species was also present in the lowland lakes of the South Island, but underwent a rapid decline, for unknown reasons, in the 19th century – the last regular breeding record in the South Island was in 1941. In 2012 a pair bred near Takaka for the first time in recent history.


Behaviour
These freshwater diving birds usually fly only at night, whilst during the day, they are always found in the water, swimming on the surface and frequently diving to feed. Thus, if they are in danger or get disturbed at daytime they do not flee by flying, but swimming or diving away. During autumn and winter, they are found forming flocks, while during the breeding season they are mostly seen in monogamous pairs. They show aggressive territorial behaviour towards intruders  and the otherwise silent species give short calls throughout the breeding season and when in danger.


Diet

Their diet consists mostly of aquatic insects and their larvae, as well as small molluscs such as freshwater snails. Bigger prey such as fish and freshwater crayfish are sometimes eaten too. Thus, their bill, being short and pointed, is adapted to their mainly invertebrate diet. They catch their prey during dives and feeding underwater or pick it from the water surface.


Breeding

The breeding season is from June to March. On average 2–3 eggs are laid and incubated 22–23 days by both female and male, in a nest. The nest is mostly made out of surrounding vegetation, including floating plant material. Hatched chicks are precocial, although being flightless for the first few weeks they can swim and dive. Both parents assist in rearing and feeding their young for up to 70 days after hatching. Until the adult plumage develops, the chick has irregular striped markings on head and neck and the bill is black.


Conservation
This species is endemic to New Zealand and is nowadays only found in the North Island. In 1994, the IUCN classified the New Zealand grebe as Endangered, but due to conservation actions including habitat management, its population has recently increased to around 1,900–2,000 birds and was reclassified as Near Threatened in 2016, and to least concern in 2022. Human activity currently has a net benefit as artificial habitat, including farm dams and ponds formed for stock water supplies, increases the area of occupation for the grebes. So, although the population is still quite small it is thought to no longer be in decline.


References


External links

Poliocephalus rufopectus discussed on RNZ Critter of the Week, 22 March 2024
New Zealand dabchick at New Zealand Birds Online

The '''New Zealand grebe''' ('''''Poliocephalus rufopectus'''''), also known as the '''New Zealand dabchick''' or '''weweia''', is a member of the [[grebe]] family [[endemic (ecology)|endemic]] to [[New Zealand]].

== Taxonomy ==
''Poliocephalus rufopectus'' is an aquatic diving bird in the [[grebe]] order.<ref name="IRTGJKNH" />

''Poliocephalus rufopectus'' (G.R.Gray) was originally described by the English ornithologist [[George Robert Gray]] as ''Podiceps rufopectus'' G.R.Gray, 1843 (in [[subgenus]] ''Poliocephalus'').<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Gray |first=George Robert |date=1843-01-01 |title=Fauna of New Zealand. List of the Birds hitertho recorded as found in New Zealand, Chatham, and Auckland Islands, with their synonyma |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q125028720 |journal=Travels in New Zealand with contributions to the geography, geology, botany, and natural history of that country |language=English |pages=186– 201}}</ref> The type specimen was collected by British botanist and scientific collector [[Andrew Sinclair (botanist)|Andrew Sinclair]] in New Zealand.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book |last=Watola |first=George |url=https://nzbirdsonline.org.nz/sites/all/files/POLRUF_Watola.pdf |title=The Discovery of New Zealand's Birds |date=2011 |publisher=Arun Books |edition=Updated and amended text supplied by George Watola, October, 2011. 3rd |location=Orewa, New Zealand |page=38 |language=English |chapter=New Zealand dabchick |archive-date=2022-01-21 |access-date=2024-03-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220121075657/https://nzbirdsonline.org.nz/sites/all/files/POLRUF_Watola.pdf |url-status=dead }}</ref> ''P. rufopectus'' is one of two species in the genus ''Poliocephalus,'' with the other being the [[hoary-headed grebe]] (''Poliocephalus poliocephalus'') which is native to Australia.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Fjeldsa |first=Jon |date=1983-09-01 |title=Social Behaviour and Displays of the Hoary-Headed Grebe Poliocephalus Poliocephalus |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q125030169 |journal=Emu |volume=83 |issue=3 |pages=129–140 |doi=10.1071/MU9830129|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Storer |first=Robert W. |date=1987-09-01 |title=Morphology and Relationships of the Hoary-headed Grebe and the New Zealand Dabchick |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q125030272 |journal=Emu |language=English |volume=87 |issue=3 |pages=150–157 |doi=10.1071/MU9870150|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Description ==
The New Zealand grebe has dark brown plumage, a small black head with fine silver feathers, a black pointed bill and characteristic yellow eyes.<ref name="PORTGFDV">{{cite book|last1=Robertson|first1=Hugh|last2=Heather|first2=Barrie|title=The Hand Guide to the Birds of New Zealand|date=1999|publisher=Penguin}}</ref><ref name="IRTGJKNH">{{cite web |title=New Zealand dabchick |url=http://www.nzbirdsonline.org.nz/species/new-zealand-dabchick |access-date=23 Mar 2024 |website=New Zealand Birds Online}}</ref> It grows to about {{Convert|29|cm|abbr=on}}, and weighs about {{Convert|250|g|abbr=on}}.<ref name="FGNZB" /> Non-breeding individuals have a paler plumage and females tend to be a bit lighter, smaller and have a slightly shorter bill than males.<ref name="PORTGFDV" /><ref name="ANZABV1">{{cite book | last = Marchant | first = S. | last2 = Higgins | first2 = P.J. (Eds.) | title = Handbook of Australian, New Zealand & Antarctic Birds. Volume 1, Ratites to ducks; Part A, Ratites to petrels | publisher = Oxford University Press | date = 1990 | location = Melbourne | page = 107}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
This grebe species inhabits mainly shallow freshwater lakes, ponds and sheltered inlets.<ref name="IRTGJKNH" />
Currently it is found mainly in the [[North Island]], where it is well distributed on the coastal lakes of the West coast from North Cape to Pukekohe and from Taranaki to Paraparaumu, as well as on ponds of the [[North Island Volcanic Plateau|Volcanic Plateau]], Gisborne, Hawkes Bay and the Wairarapa.<ref name="PORTGFDV" /><ref name="FGNZB"/><ref>{{Cite web |last=commoncopper |date=April 2022 |title=New Zealand Dabchick (Poliocephalus rufopectus) |url=https://inaturalist.nz/observations/112250817 |access-date=2022-04-22 |website=iNaturalist NZ |language=en}}</ref> 
Formerly this species was also present in the lowland lakes of the [[South Island]],<ref name="WNZB">Andrew Crowe, ''"Which New Zealand Bird?"'', Penguin, 2001</ref> but underwent a rapid decline, for unknown reasons, in the 19th century – the last regular breeding record in the South Island was in 1941.<ref name="FGNZB">Barrie Heather and Hugh Robertson, ''"The Field Guide to the Birds of New Zealand (revised edition)"'', Viking, 2005</ref> In 2012 a pair bred near Takaka for the first time in recent history.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Petyt |first=Chris |date=2013-01-01 |title=First recent recorded breeding of the New Zealand dabchick (Poliocephalus rufopectus) in the South Island |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q125030354 |journal=Notornis |language=English |volume=60 |pages=322–323}}</ref>

== Behaviour ==
These freshwater diving birds usually fly only at night, whilst during the day, they are always found in the water, swimming on the surface and frequently diving to feed. Thus, if they are in danger or get disturbed at daytime they do not flee by flying, but swimming or diving away. During autumn and winter, they are found forming flocks, while during the breeding season they are mostly seen in [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous pairs]]. They show aggressive territorial behaviour towards intruders <ref name="PORTGFDV" /><ref name="IRTGJKNH" /> and the otherwise silent species give short calls throughout the breeding season and when in danger.<ref name="IRTGJKNH" /><ref name="OORRRNVKDL">{{cite book|last1=Chambers|first1=S.|title=Birds of New Zealand - Locality Guide|date=2009|publisher=Arun Books|location=Orewa, New Zealand|edition=3rd}}</ref>

== Diet ==
[[File:Adult Grebe with a tasty bug in it's mouth.jpg|thumb|230px|Adult grebe with insect]]
Their diet consists mostly of aquatic insects and their larvae, as well as small molluscs such as freshwater snails.<ref name="WNZB"/> Bigger prey such as fish and freshwater crayfish are sometimes eaten too.<ref name="FGNZB"/> Thus, their bill, being short and pointed, is adapted to their mainly invertebrate diet. They catch their prey during dives and feeding underwater or pick it from the water surface.<ref name="PORTGFDV" /><ref name="IRTGJKNH" />

== Breeding ==
[[File:A dabchick with her young chick nestled in amongst her feathers 01.jpg|thumb|230px|Adult with young chick]]
[[File:New Zealand grebe at Queen Elizabeth Park - MC 03.jpg|thumb|230px|Adult and chick at [[Queen Elizabeth Park, Kāpiti Coast|Queen Elizabeth Park]], [[Kāpiti Coast District]]]]
The breeding season is from June to March. On average 2–3 eggs are laid and incubated 22–23 days by both female and male, in a nest. The nest is mostly made out of surrounding vegetation, including floating plant material. Hatched chicks are [[precocial]], although being flightless for the first few weeks they can swim and dive.<ref name="PORTGFDV" /> Both parents assist in rearing and feeding their young for up to 70 days after hatching. Until the adult plumage develops, the chick has irregular striped markings on head and neck and the bill is black.<ref name="IRTGJKNH" />

== Conservation ==
This species is [[endemic]] to New Zealand and is nowadays only found in the North Island. In 1994, the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]] classified the New Zealand grebe as Endangered, but due to conservation actions including habitat management, its population has recently increased to around 1,900–2,000 birds and was reclassified as Near Threatened in 2016, and to least concern in 2022.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /> Human activity currently has a net benefit as artificial habitat, including farm dams and ponds formed for stock water supplies, increases the area of occupation for the grebes.<ref name="PORTGFDV" /><ref name="IRTGJKNH" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Bright |first=A. |last2=Waas |first2=J. |last3=Innes |first3=J. |date=2004-01-01 |title=Correlations between human-made structures, boat-pass frequency and the number of New Zealand dabchicks (Poliocephalus rufopectus) on the Rotorua Lakes, New Zealand |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q125030300 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Ecology |language=English |volume=28 |issue=1 |pages=137–142}}</ref> So, although the population is still quite small it is thought to no longer be in decline.<ref name="TRGDFJD">{{cite web|title=New Zealand Grebe (Poliocephalus rufopectus) - BirdLife species factsheet|url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/22696592|website=datazone.birdlife.org|access-date=12 April 2017}}</ref>
